element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Player learns club distance/angle mechanics before attempting holes
Straightforward fairway layouts teach aiming and power control
Players apply club selection knowledge to navigate hazards
Combined mechanics demand precise club/angle judgment

typical_tension_triggers: Tight margin for error on dog-leg
rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Turn-based sport with player-controlled pacing; tension builds through hole difficulty progression within 18-hole structure

difficulty_curve.early_game: Straightforward holes introducing basic mechanics and club selection
difficulty_curve.mid_game: Introduction of hazards (bunkers, water) requiring tactical club/angle decisions
difficulty_curve.late_game: Complex layouts combining elevation changes, doglegs, and multiple hazard types

notable_level_patterns.description: Standard golf format with progressive difficulty; early holes establish mechanics, mid-course introduces obstacles, late holes demand mastery of combined systems
Environmental obstacles (bunkers, water) introduced gradually; early courses have simple layouts, later courses stack multiple hazard types
Holes progress from straight fairways to doglegs and elevation changes, forcing repeated tactical decision-making

sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor - Golf NES/FDS article)
limitations: Source material focuses on technical/regional differences rather than level design sequencing. Element introduction order, rhythm patterns, and spatial grammar inferred from golf genre conventions and sports-racing paradigm rather than explicit design documentation. No detailed level-by-level breakdown available in provided source.
